sections.heading: Mechanism 1.2 — PSP orchestration and local-rail routing
sections.text: No single scheme covers 14 countries economically. Kepler orchestrates across card schemes, local instant-payment rails (UPI in India, PIX in Brazil, PayNow in Singapore), and bank-transfer/RTP networks, routing each transaction to the cheapest rail that clears the risk bar. The mechanism chains directly out of 1.1: because the ledger is unified, the router can make a portfolio decision — accept slightly higher fraud on a low-margin D3 courier payout routed over a cheap local rail, because the same user's D1 ride history (fed in via S-402) collateralises the risk. Trace S-403 captures the routing economics: shifting 22 points of volume from card schemes to local rails cut blended cost-of-payment from 1.9% to 1.3% of TPV without raising realised fraud, because the risk headroom came from cross-domain data, not from the rail itself.

sections.heading: Mechanism 1.3 — Cash-free closure of D1 and D3
sections.text: Here Driver 1 hands off to the flywheel. Once the rails are unified and cheap, Kepler can make mobility and logistics cash-free — no driver handling notes, no courier reconciling coins. This is the first cross-domain coupling: removing cash raises D1's interaction density (D) because more low-value rides become viable, and raises D3's network liquidity (L) because couriers get paid instantly and stay on the platform. Trace S-404: in the six cities where cash was fully removed, D1 ride frequency rose 11% and D3 courier churn fell 9pp — neither number belongs to Payments' P&L, which is precisely the point. Payments spent margin to buy density and liquidity for other domains, and the moat identity M rose even though Domain 4's standalone contribution looked flat. A conglomerate scorecard would have killed this trade.

sections.heading: Driver 2 — Credit & underwriting: lending on data no bank can see
sections.text: If the rails make Kepler a payments company, the credit engine makes it a fintech. Driver 2 takes the unified ledger from Driver 1 and does the thing banks cannot: it underwrites 28.4m actives using behavioural collateral — how reliably you ride, charge, deliver, and pay — rather than a thin or absent credit file. This is where T compounds into monetization, because trust that is measured can be lent against.

sections.heading: Mechanism 2.1 — Behavioural underwriting off D1 and D3 exhaust
sections.text:
The core mechanism: Kepler scores creditworthiness from platform behaviour that is exogenous to any credit bureau. A D3 courier with 18 months of on-time deliveries and stable D1 commuting patterns is a better risk than her thin bureau file suggests. This is the second cross-domain coupling — Payments underwrites on D1 mobility regularity and D3 logistics reliability. Trace S-405 documents the lift: adding cross-domain behavioural features cut the Gini-normalised default rate 210bps at constant approval, or equivalently raised approvals 14% at constant loss. The scoring logic lives in D6's feature store but is owned, productised, and priced by D4.

Trace S-406 is the query itself; note the thick_file flag — a user is "thick" not because a bureau has data on them but because Kepler does, across domains. That is the underwriting moat in one boolean.
code: -- S-406: Kepler cross-domain credit score (nightly batch, feature-store backed)WITH mobility AS (   -- D1 signal: regularity + tenure  SELECT user_id,         COUNT(*)                         AS rides_90d,         STDDEV(hour_of_day)              AS commute_regularity,   -- lower = more predictable         DATE_PART('day', NOW() - MIN(ts)) AS tenure_days  FROM d1_rides WHERE ts > NOW() - INTERVAL '90 days'  GROUP BY user_id),logistics AS (       -- D3 signal: earnings stability + reliability  SELECT courier_id AS user_id,         AVG(on_time_flag)::numeric(4,3)  AS on_time_rate,         STDDEV(daily_earnings)           AS earnings_vol,         SUM(completed_jobs)              AS jobs_90d  FROM d3_courier_jobs WHERE ts > NOW() - INTERVAL '90 days'  GROUP BY courier_id),payments AS (        -- D4 signal: repayment + ledger balance behaviour  SELECT account_id AS user_id,         AVG(repaid_on_time)::numeric(4,3) AS repay_rate,         MIN(running_balance)              AS min_balance_90d,         COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE type='chargeback') AS cb_90d  FROM ledger_entries WHERE ts > NOW() - INTERVAL '90 days'  GROUP BY account_id)SELECT u.user_id,       ROUND( 560         + 90 * COALESCE(l.on_time_rate,0.5)          -- reliability         + 70 * LEAST(m.tenure_days/365.0, 1.0)       -- tenure, capped 1yr         - 55 * COALESCE(m.commute_regularity,4)/8.0  -- erratic = riskier         - 40 * LEAST(p.cb_90d, 3)                     -- chargebacks penalised         + 60 * COALESCE(p.repay_rate,0.5)             -- prior repayment       )::int AS kepler_score,       (l.on_time_rate IS NOT NULL AND m.rides_90d > 20) AS thick_fileFROM users uLEFT JOIN mobility m  USING (user_id)LEFT JOIN logistics l USING (user_id)LEFT JOIN payments  p USING (user_id);

sections.heading: Mechanism 2.2 — Working-capital and instalment products that recycle into the flywheel
sections.text: The score from 2.1 powers three products: courier working-capital advances (D3), device instalments (D5), and consumer BNPL on rides/charges (D1/D2). The chaining matters: a device instalment (D5) puts a proprietary-sensor device (which raises data breadth B) into a user's hands who could not have paid upfront — so credit manufactures moat inputs for other domains. Trace S-407: instalment financing raised D5 device attach among the bottom two income quartiles by 31%, directly feeding D5's breadth term. Credit here is not a yield product; it is a distribution subsidy for the moat, repaid by better data.

sections.heading: Mechanism 2.3 — Portfolio risk and the loss-provisioning discipline
sections.text: Underwriting on behaviour is powerful and pro-cyclical — the same D1/D3 signals that predict good times can turn together in a downturn. Driver 2 therefore hands a risk to Driver 3: correlated behavioural default. Trace S-408 sets the guardrail — exposure is capped per cohort and provisioned against a stressed scenario where D1 ride frequency and D3 job volume fall together (the correlation the cross-domain data itself reveals). The credit engine's edge and its systemic risk come from the same source, which is the honest version of the thesis.

sections.heading: Driver 3 — Trust, fraud & compliance: the term-owner mechanism for T
sections.text: This is the driver that literally moves T in the moat identity. Fraud losses and compliance failures are trust leaks; every prevented fraud and every clean audit is trust retained and re-lent to all six domains. Driver 3 takes the credit exposure from Driver 2 and the payment flows from Driver 1 and defends both — using signals it does not itself generate.

sections.heading: Mechanism 3.1 — Real-time fraud scoring on D6 signals fed by D1 and D5
sections.text:
The fraud engine scores every transaction in the ~40ms RISK node from Driver 1's architecture. Its features are the third and fourth cross-domain couplings: fraud signals from D6, which are themselves assembled from D1 location telemetry (is this ride's device physically where the card says it is?) and D5 device fingerprints (is this the same hardware that onboarded?). A card-not-present transaction that geolocates 300km from the user's D1 device is scored differently from one that sits inside their normal mobility envelope. Trace S-409: cross-domain fraud features cut fraud basis points from 18 to 7 bps of TPV while lowering the false-positive decline rate 22% — trust up on both sides of the ledger, which is the rare win where fewer good customers are wrongly blocked and fewer bad ones get through.

Trace S-410 is the config. Two design choices carry the thesis: the default response to elevated risk is step-up, not decline (preserve the good customer, protect trust on the acceptance side), and the trusted_corridor override spends earned trust to remove friction — the direct, mechanical expression of T as a compounding asset. The courier_collusion rule is pure cross-domain: it can only exist because D3 payouts and D4 funding sources sit in one ledger.
code: # S-410: fraud-ops rules config (evaluated after model score, pre-authorization)version: 2026.7model:  name: d6_txn_fraud_v11          # served by D6, features from D1/D5/D4  score_field: p_fraud  latency_budget_ms: 40thresholds:  auto_approve:  { p_fraud: "< 0.02" }  step_up_3ds:   { p_fraud: "0.02 – 0.15" }   # challenge, don't decline  manual_review: { p_fraud: "0.15 – 0.45" }  auto_decline:  { p_fraud: ">= 0.45" }rules:  - id: geo_velocity    when: "d1_last_ping_km_from_txn > 250 AND minutes_since_ping < 30"    action: step_up_3ds    note: "physically impossible travel vs D1 telemetry"  - id: device_swap    when: "d5_device_fingerprint != account.enrolled_fingerprint"    action: manual_review  - id: courier_collusion    when: "d3_payout_to == d4_card_funding_source AND velocity_24h > 8"    action: hold_and_investigate     # self-dealing loop across D3+D4  - id: new_account_high_ticket    when: "account.age_days < 7 AND txn.amount > 400"    action: step_up_3dsoverrides:  trusted_corridor:    when: "kepler_score > 720 AND thick_file == true"    action: relax_thresholds         # earned trust buys friction reductionreview_queue: { sla_minutes: 15, staffing: follow_the_sun }

sections.heading: Mechanism 3.2 — Compliance, licensing, and the 14-country perimeter
sections.text: Trust is also regulatory. Kepler holds or passports payment/e-money and lending licences across 14 countries, and Driver 3 owns the reconciliation and reporting node from the architecture. The mechanism chains from 3.1: the same identity graph that powers fraud scoring powers KYC/KYB and sanctions screening, so compliance is a reuse of the trust infrastructure, not a parallel cost. Trace S-411: consolidating KYC across domains (one verified identity reused by D1, D3, D4, D5) cut per-user onboarding cost 38% and — more importantly — made the identity itself a moat asset, because a once-verified Kepler user onboards into every new domain with zero incremental friction. That is trust as distribution.

sections.heading: Driver 4 — Monetization & interchange: turning a high M into cash
sections.text: The final driver extracts money from the trust the first three built. It takes the defended, underwritten, cash-free flows and monetises them through interchange, take-rates, credit yield, and — increasingly — data-adjacent products. The strategic subtlety: interchange is regulated and varies by region, so the monetization ceiling is set by policy, not ambition, and the durable margin comes from the parts of the stack regulators do not cap.

sections.heading: Mechanism 4.1 — Interchange capture within regulated caps
sections.text:
Regulatory / interchange NOTE — S-412. Interchange is asymmetrically capped across Kepler's footprint, and the monetization model must respect it region-by-region:

EU/EEA: hard caps under the Interchange Fee Regulation — 0.20% debit, 0.30% credit. Little room; monetize on volume and adjacencies, not rate.

Singapore / much of SE Asia: no hard statutory cap but scheme-set and competitive; effective blended ~0.5–0.9%.

India (UPI): merchant-side MDR on UPI is effectively zero by policy — monetization here is never interchange; it is credit, float, and value-added services.

Brazil (PIX): instant-payment rail with no interchange; Central Bank actively compresses card economics. Same conclusion as India.

US-style uncapped credit interchange (~1.8–2.2%) does not exist in Kepler's markets — the model must not assume it. Implication: in half of Kepler's TPV, interchange is legally near-zero. Monetization therefore cannot rest on the swipe; it must rest on trust-adjacent products (credit yield, insurance, merchant lending, data-informed underwriting) that regulators do not cap. Trace S-412 is the reason Driver 2 exists.

This note is why Payments cannot be a monoline. In its two largest-by-population markets the swipe is free by law, so the only monetization path is the one that requires the whole flywheel: lending and services priced off cross-domain trust.

sections.heading: Mechanism 4.2 — Take-rate, float, and the blended monetization stack
sections.text:
Kepler monetizes across four layers, each with a different regulatory exposure. The table makes the stack legible and shows where margin is durable.

Trace S-413: the two high-durability layers (credit yield and float/services) already contribute 61% of Payments gross profit despite being under 40% of TPV — evidence that the monetization has already migrated off the regulated swipe toward the trust-moated products. The statement mockup below is where the user experiences all four layers at once.

The mockup is a trust artefact as much as a financial one: the score is visible and moving, the APR fell because behaviour earned it (the credit engine talking to the customer), and the fraud line advertises that protection ran without wrongly blocking her. The statement is where T is made felt. Trace S-414 notes that surfacing the earned-APR line lifted repeat credit uptake 17% — trust, once shown, sells the next product.
tables: Credit yield (BNPL, courier WC, D5 instalments)

sections.heading: Mechanism 4.3 — The ledger as monetization evidence
sections.text:
Underneath the statement sits the raw ledger — the same one from Driver 1, now read as a monetization record. The table below shows how a single day's mixed flows carry different margins, and how the join (one user, many domains) is what makes the portfolio work.

Trace S-415: read across the user column and the monetization thesis appears — rivera_a and courier_301 each touch three domains in one day, and the high-margin entries (credit yield at 700–1240 bps) are only underwritable because the low-margin entries (rides, jobs) supplied the behavioural collateral. The cheap flows are the moat; the expensive flows are the monetization; the ledger is the join that makes one pay for the other. Trace S-416 closes the loop: contribution per interaction inside Payments moved from −0.19 over the trailing period, the clearest single proof point that the flywheel's target path from −0.31 blended is achievable — and that it runs through Domain 4.

sections.heading: What's true vs what counts
sections.text:
What's true. Payments is the fastest-growing domain (+58% yoy), it now earns most of its gross profit from durable, un-capped layers (S-413), and its per-interaction contribution has already turned positive (S-416). Interchange is legally near-zero across half of Kepler's TPV (S-412), so the standalone "payments company" framing is not just incomplete — it is impossible in these markets. On a monoline basis Kepler Payments would trail a focused regional PSP on rate and a focused lender on scale.

What counts. None of the durable margin exists without the other five domains. The credit engine underwrites on D1 mobility regularity and D3 courier reliability (S-405, S-406); the fraud engine defends on D6 signals built from D1 telemetry and D5 fingerprints (S-409, S-410); the rails close the loop by making D1 and D3 cash-free, buying density and liquidity that never show up on Payments' own P&L (S-404). Payments is the term-owner for trust (T) — the one term every other domain multiplies against in M = D × B × L × T — and it is the only domain that converts a high M into cash the balance sheet can see. Run it as a sixth P&L and you cap it at regulated interchange in a world that is legislating interchange toward zero. Sequence it as the flywheel's closing device — underwriting on four domains' data, defended by a fifth, feeding trust back to all six — and it becomes the mechanism by which the moat, finally, gets paid. The swipe is what's true. The trust is what counts.

sections.heading: Domain 5 — Devices & Hardware: we sell hardware to harvest data no one else has
sections.text:
Devices is the domain most often misread as the weakest link in the Kepler portfolio, and the misreading is instructive. On a standalone basis it is a low-margin consumer-electronics and industrial-sensor business — a category the market has spent two decades learning to value at single-digit multiples, priced on unit economics, channel, and refresh cycles. Read that way, Devices is a drag: it consumes working capital, it carries inventory risk, it lives and dies on a bill of materials measured in cents, and it will never out-margin a payments network or out-scale a mobility fleet. If Kepler were six businesses, Devices would be the one a focus-minded activist spins out first.

That is exactly why it is in the portfolio, and exactly why it is misunderstood. In the moat identity

Devices is the domain that owns data breadth (B) — the width of the sensor manifold that every other domain's models draw from. Density (D) is how often the physical world touches Kepler; breadth (B) is how many independent dimensions it measures each time it does. Mobility (D1) already gives Kepler the densest location-and-behaviour stream on Earth, but density on a narrow set of channels is a shallow moat: a competitor with GPS and a payment rail can approximate most of it. Breadth is what cannot be approximated. When a Kepler device measures road-surface vibration at 400 Hz, ambient particulate load, battery-cell impedance under real load, grip torque on a courier's handlebar, or the acoustic signature of a failing charger relay, it is capturing signal no competitor has instrumented and none can synthesise after the fact. The hardware is not the product. The hardware is the sampling apparatus, and the sample is the product.

This reframes the whole P&L. We do not sell devices to make money on devices; the gross margin on the hardware is a means of financing a proprietary data-acquisition network that would otherwise sit on Kepler's cost line at full expense. Every unit we ship into a vehicle, a courier's kit, a charging bay, or a merchant's counter is a paid-for sensor node in a fleet that sharpens D6's models — which in turn lower cost and raise trust across D1–D4. The cross-feeds run in every direction, and three matter most: the sensor manifold's breadth (B) directly sharpens the D6 models that improve routing, pricing, and fraud detection; Devices ride physically inside D1 vehicles and D3 courier kit, giving them a distribution channel and power source no standalone device maker enjoys; and D4 payments run on-device, closing the monetisation loop at the point of physical interaction while D2 energy telemetry flows back from every charger and battery we instrument. The rest of this section develops four drivers — the product line, the BOM and margin structure, the supply chain, and the sensor data that is the real product — and shows how each mechanism chains into the next.
code: M  =  Interaction density (D)  ×  Data breadth (B)  ×  Network liquidity (L)  ×  Trust (T)

sections.heading: Driver 1 — The product line: four form factors, one sensor manifold
sections.text: The product strategy is deliberately not a consumer-electronics strategy. Kepler builds the minimum number of form factors required to place its sensor manifold into the maximum number of high-signal physical contexts. There are four, and they are designed as one modular platform rather than four products.

sections.heading: Mechanism 1.1 — The manifold-first design rule (S-501)
sections.text:
Every Kepler device is architected around a shared sensor-and-compute core — the Kepler Sensor Module (KSM) — with form-factor-specific carrier boards and enclosures wrapped around it (S-501). The KSM carries the inertial, environmental, and acoustic sensors plus an edge-inference NPU; the carrier adds the connectivity, power, and human-interface elements each context needs. This is the opposite of the consumer playbook, where each product is bespoke to hit a price point. Kepler accepts a slightly higher per-unit cost in exchange for a single validated sensor stack that produces dimensionally consistent data across every context — a road-vibration reading from a vehicle mount and from a courier's handlebar are directly comparable because they come from the same silicon and the same calibration. That comparability is what makes the breadth (B) additive rather than fragmented, and it is the design decision that most directly serves D6.

The four form factors:

The Kepler Beacon (KB-1) is the flagship consumer-facing device — a palm-sized navigation-and-payments companion that rides on a vehicle dash or a courier's bar, described in the mockup below. The Kepler Node (KN-2) is an embedded industrial module designed to be built directly into D1 vehicles and D2 chargers at the point of manufacture — no enclosure, no screen, pure sensing and telemetry. The Kepler Tag (KT-3) is a low-cost adhesive asset-tracker for D3 logistics parcels and courier kit, harvesting shock, tilt, and temperature across the delivery graph. The Kepler Counter (KC-4) is a merchant-facing payment-and-sensing terminal that anchors D4 at the physical point of sale.

sections.heading: Mechanism 1.2 — The product mockup: what the Beacon actually shows (S-502)
sections.text:
The KB-1's value to the user has to be real, or the device never gets placed and the sensor never samples. The interface is therefore designed as a genuinely useful navigation-and-payments surface whose data-harvesting is invisible (S-502). Rendered:

Every element on that screen is a cross-domain coupling made visible to the user. The turn card is D1's routing model rendered locally; the "4 min to charge" and live bay price is D2 telemetry; "TAP TO PAY $12.40" is the D4 wallet executing a transaction on the device's secure element; and the air-quality and road-surface read-outs are the D5 manifold showing the user a sliver of what it is measuring — enough to feel useful, nowhere near the full sample. The design principle (S-503): give the user one visible reason to keep the device powered and mounted for every invisible stream we are collecting behind it. A device in a drawer samples nothing; retention of the hardware in position is the true north-star metric for Devices, not units sold.

sections.heading: Driver 2 — BOM, cost & margin: financing a sensor network with hardware margin
sections.text: If the product line decides what we place and where, the cost structure decides whether the placement is affordable at fleet scale. Devices runs on a deliberately unusual margin philosophy: hardware margin exists to subsidise sensor coverage, not to maximise device profit.

sections.heading: Mechanism 2.1 — The BOM cost table and the teardown that names the sensors (S-504, S-505)
sections.text:
The KB-1 Beacon's bill of materials is where the strategy becomes concrete. The costed BOM (S-504), at a 2m-unit annual run-rate, landed cost per unit:

The teardown / exploded view (S-505) names where each of those sits physically, because the placement of a sensor determines the quality of the sample:

The exploded view carries a decision the BOM table hides (S-506): the environmental intake vent is engineered to sit above the compute stack, thermally isolated, so the SEN-55 reads true ambient particulate rather than the device's own heat plume — a $0.40 tooling cost that protects the fidelity of a data stream worth far more than the device. The IMU is bonded rigidly to the mount interface so vibration couples through the chassis of the D1 vehicle or D3 handlebar it rides on, turning the whole vehicle into an extension of the sensor. These are the design choices that separate a sensor that samples from one that merely reports.

sections.heading: Mechanism 2.2 — The margin waterfall: ASP to gross margin, and why it is thin on purpose (S-507)
sections.text:
The margin waterfall from average selling price to gross margin (S-507), per KB-1 unit:

A 31% hardware gross margin is unremarkable for consumer electronics and would be a disappointment for any other Kepler domain. Held against the standalone frame, Devices looks like the portfolio's laggard. But the waterfall is measuring the wrong thing (S-508). The strategically correct denominator is not device profit; it is the fully-loaded cost of the proprietary data the device generates over its service life. A KB-1 in position for its ~30-month life emits on the order of tens of millions of sensor-seconds across five independent modalities. Financing that acquisition through a standalone sensor-network build would sit at full expense on D6's cost line — which today runs at −$420m as a cost centre. Instead, the hardware margin pre-pays it: the device sells at a positive gross margin, and the data arrives effectively free to D6. The correct read of the waterfall is not "31% margin" but "a proprietary sensor network delivered at negative net acquisition cost." That is the number the board should track, and it is the bridge into Driver 4.

sections.heading: Driver 3 — The supply chain: geography as a sensor-coverage decision
sections.text: A sensor network is only as broad as the units you can actually manufacture, land, and place. The supply chain is therefore not a back-office function in Devices — it is the throttle on how fast breadth (B) compounds, and it is managed as such.

sections.heading: Mechanism 3.1 — The supply-chain map: fabs, assembly, ports (S-509)
sections.text:
The physical flow (S-509), from silicon to placement:

Two structural choices are load-bearing (S-510). First, final assembly is concentrated at Jabil Penang with ASE Kaohsiung doing SoC packaging and test — deliberately dual-region against the fab base but single-node on final assembly, trading resilience for the tighter process control that a consistent sensor calibration demands. Second, the placement leg is Kepler's own: units land at the PSA Singapore and Rotterdam hubs and then ride the D1 mobility fleet and D3 courier network into the field, rather than through third-party retail. This is the distribution coupling that a standalone device maker cannot replicate — Kepler already has vehicles and couriers moving through all 42 cities daily, so the last mile of device placement is nearly free and rides infrastructure that already exists.

sections.heading: Mechanism 3.2 — The manufacturing Gantt and the concentration risk it exposes (S-511, S-512)
sections.text:
The ramp for the KB-1 v2 and the embedded KN-2 (S-511), quarters across FY26–FY27:

The Gantt makes one dependency unmissable (S-512): the KN-2 embedded module's mass production is gated on the D1 vehicle manufacturing line, because the Node is built into vehicles at their point of assembly, not retrofitted. This is the cross-domain coupling expressed as a schedule constraint — Devices cannot ramp its highest-breadth embedded sensor faster than Mobility ramps the vehicles it lives inside. The strategic reading is that Devices' breadth-compounding is paced by D1's fleet growth, which is precisely why sequencing (the governing thought) matters: place the sensor line and the vehicle line out of sync and the moat forms slower than the model requires. The concentration risk on Jabil Penang and the single Singapore port hub is the other edge-case the Gantt surfaces — a typhoon-season disruption at Klang or Penang is a direct hit to breadth, so the FY27 plan carries a qualified second final-assembly node as insurance, accepted as a margin cost against sensor-coverage continuity.

sections.heading: Driver 4 — Proprietary sensor data is the real product
sections.text: Everything above — the four form factors, the thin-on-purpose margin, the fleet-ridden supply chain — exists to serve this driver. Devices' claim on the moat is breadth (B), and this is where breadth becomes measurable value.

sections.heading: Mechanism 4.1 — The five-modality manifold and its uniqueness (S-513)
sections.text: The Kepler manifold captures five broad, mutually-reinforcing modalities that no competitor has jointly instrumented at fleet scale (S-513): kinematic (400 Hz 6-axis IMU — road roughness, grip torque, crash signatures), environmental (particulate, VOC, humidity — air-quality maps street-by-street), acoustic (MEMS array — failing-relay and bearing signatures for predictive maintenance), positional (dual-band GNSS at sub-metre — lane-level, not road-level), and electrical (battery-cell impedance and charger telemetry via the KN-2). The uniqueness is not any single stream — competitors buy IMUs and GNSS too — but the joint distribution: only Kepler observes all five from the same calibrated node, time-aligned, across the same 42-city fleet. That joint distribution is the raw material breadth is made of, and it is why the sample cannot be reconstructed after the fact by anyone lacking the placed hardware.

sections.heading: Mechanism 4.2 — How breadth sharpens D6, and the value attribution (S-514, S-515)
sections.text:
The manifold's payoff is realised in D6's models, and the couplings are specific (S-514). Kinematic road-roughness data sharpens D1's routing and ride-comfort models and feeds D2's siting (rough corridors correlate with battery-drain hotspots worth charger placement). Acoustic fault signatures drive predictive maintenance across the D1 fleet and D2 charger network, converting reactive downtime into scheduled service. Electrical telemetry from the KN-2 feeds D2's battery-health and dispatch pricing. And the on-device secure element plus placement context feed D4's fraud model — a payment tap whose device reports an impossible kinematic trajectory is a fraud signal no card network can see. The second data table quantifies the attributed lift (S-515), Kepler's internal model-improvement attribution from the sensor manifold, FY26 estimate:

The attribution table is the answer to the board's fair challenge — "is the compounding real or a story?" Each row is a coupling the governing thought predicts, now carrying a number. Breadth (B) is not an abstraction; it is a −18% downtime figure and an −11% fraud false-positive figure that land on other domains' P&Ls (S-516). The edge-case worth naming: the environmental modality produces a saleable data product (street-level air quality to municipalities), the one place Devices' data monetises directly rather than only through internal model lift — a hedge that gives the breadth thesis an external price signal to validate against.

sections.heading: What's true vs what counts
sections.text:
What's true. Devices is, on its own financials, the portfolio's least impressive business. A 31% hardware gross margin, real inventory and tariff risk, a supply chain with genuine concentration exposure at Penang and Singapore, and a ramp paced by the domains it depends on. Judged as a device company against a focused device company, it trails — exactly as the sum-of-the-parts frame predicts, and exactly the kind of business an activist would tell Kepler to spin out. All of that is true and none of it should be argued away.

What counts. Devices does not exist to make money on devices; it exists to place a proprietary five-modality sensor manifold into the physical world at negative net data-acquisition cost, and to own breadth (B) in the moat identity — the one term no competitor can reconstruct after the fact, because it requires hardware already placed and already sampling. The margin waterfall's real denominator is the fully-loaded cost of data D6 would otherwise expense; on that denominator, a 31% gross margin is a sensor network delivered for free. The couplings are not aspirational: kinematic data sharpens D1 routing and D2 siting, acoustic data cuts fleet downtime 18%, electrical telemetry lifts D2 throughput, and on-device D4 payments plus placement context cut fraud false-positives 11% — each a measured lift on another domain's P&L. Run Devices as a device P&L and you optimise the margin, spin it out, and starve the moat of its widest input. Sequence it as the flywheel's sampling apparatus — paced deliberately against D1's vehicle line and D3's courier network so breadth compounds in step — and it becomes the domain that makes the other five un-catchable. The hardware is the cost of admission. The data is the business.

sections.heading: Domain 6 — Data & AI Platform: the moat is not a business, it is the hub of the flywheel
sections.text:
Every other domain section in this document ends by naming which of the four moat terms it moves and which domains it feeds. Domain 6 is where those threads are tied. It is the only domain that does not own a customer, a vehicle, a charger, a courier, or a card — and it is the only domain whose output is the moat identity itself. Read as a P&L it is an embarrassment: a −$420m cost centre, the single largest line item the conglomerate-discount bears say proves Kepler is undisciplined. Read as the spine, it is the reason the other five stop being five. Domain 6 owns and compounds data breadth (B) directly — it is the layer that turns proprietary sensor and behaviour exhaust into features no competitor can assemble — and it is the only layer that touches all four terms of

because it is the multiplication. D1 produces density, D3 produces liquidity, D2 and D4 produce trust, D5 produces breadth — but M is a product, not a sum, and a product only exists once the terms are computed together on shared features. That computation is Domain 6. The cross-feeds are unusually literal here: Domain 6 ingests D1 location traces, D5 sensor telemetry, D3 marketplace supply–demand events, and D4 fraud/settlement signals, and it serves models back — routing, siting, matching, scoring, safety — that raise D1's density, D2's reliability, D3's liquidity, and D4's approval rates. The flywheel is not a metaphor Domain 6 illustrates; it is the graph Domain 6 runs. This section opens the hub, walks its four drivers, and closes on what is settled versus what the next decade is decided by.
code: Moat strength  M  =  Interaction density (D)  ×  Data breadth (B)  ×  Network liquidity (L)  ×  Trust (T)

sections.heading: Driver 1 — The platform architecture: one ingestion spine, one feature store, one serving layer
sections.text: The first driver is the least glamorous and the most decisive: there is exactly one data platform, not six. The conglomerate failure mode is not strategic disagreement — it is six teams each standing up their own warehouse, their own feature definitions, their own model registry, so that "rider dwell time" means one thing in D1 and a subtly different thing in D3, and the join that would have produced a feature never happens. Domain 6's architecture exists to make that divergence structurally impossible.

sections.heading: Mechanism 1.1 — A single ingestion spine that normalises six event streams into one grammar
sections.text:
Every physical-world interaction across the estate emits an event: a ride start, a charge session, a courier accept, a card authorisation, a device heartbeat. S-601 — all six domains publish to a single append-only event bus (Kepler calls it the Interaction Log) under one canonical schema: entity_id, geohash, t, domain, event_type, payload, trust_context. At 28.4m MAU across 42 cities, the spine ingests ~3.9bn events/day at peak. The architecture diagram below is the physical shape of the platform; every later mechanism is a path through it.

The point of one grammar is the point-in-time join (S-602): any feature can be reconstructed as it was known at decision time, so a fraud model never trains on settlement data that only existed after the authorisation. That single discipline is what lets D4 signals safely sharpen D1–D3 without leakage — a cross-feed that is impossible in a six-warehouse world because the timestamps never reconcile.

sections.heading: Mechanism 1.2 — A feature store that is the physical form of data breadth (B)
sections.text:
The Interaction Log is raw; the feature store (S-603) is where breadth B actually lives. It holds two mirrored surfaces: an offline store (columnar, for training) and an online store (key-value, sub-10ms, for serving), guaranteeing train/serve parity. A feature computed once — say geohash_demand_z_15m, the 15-minute demand z-score for a location — is available to D1 routing, D2 siting, and D3 matching from the same row. This is the mechanism by which B compounds instead of fragmenting: each new domain does not add a warehouse, it adds columns other domains can read. The schema below is the load-bearing tables.

Note feat_entity.trust_score and feat_entity.pay_default_pd: those columns are written by D4 and read by D1 (to decide which riders can go cash-free) — the payments-closes-the-loop coupling, made concrete as two columns in one table.

sections.heading: Mechanism 1.3 — A serving layer that returns models to the domains that fed them
sections.text: Breadth is inert until it is served back. The serving layer (S-604) exposes every model behind one low-latency API with a shared contract, so a domain requests a decision (route, site, match, authorise, flag) and receives it in <60ms p95 without knowing which features or which upstream domains produced it. This is the architectural expression of "all five draw from the hub." It also means a model improvement — better ETA residuals from richer D5 telemetry, say — propagates to every consumer at once, rather than being re-implemented five times. Driver 1 has now built the pipes; Driver 2 explains what flows through them and why it compounds.

sections.heading: Driver 2 — The data flywheel: how five domains' data compounds into one moat
sections.text: The architecture is necessary but not sufficient. A pipe that carries six streams to six destinations is still a conglomerate with good plumbing. The flywheel is the claim that each domain's data raises the value of every other domain's data super-additively — that breadth B is not the sum of six datasets but their product on shared keys. Driver 2 is the mechanism of that compounding, and its centrepiece component is the Sankey and the flywheel network graph.

sections.heading: Mechanism 2.1 — Cross-domain joins create features no single domain could hold
sections.text:
The compounding is concrete. S-605: D1 location traces + D5 accelerometer telemetry + D3 courier GPS join on geohash × t to produce road_roughness and curb_availability features — signals that improve D1 routing and D3 drop-off ETA and D2 charger-siting (rough approach roads depress utilisation). No competitor with only a rideshare app, or only a delivery app, can compute that feature, because none holds all three streams on one key. S-606: D4 authorisation outcomes join to D1 rider history and D5 device fingerprint to lift the fraud model — a card decline pattern becomes explicable when the same entity's mobility regularity and device health are columns in the same row. The data-flow Sankey shows where breadth accumulates: the six sources are the left band, the moat is the single right node, and the ribbon widths are the share of the moat's feature value each source contributes.

That last line is the whole argument in one number. The +19% cross-join uplift is feature value that literally cannot exist for a focused specialist, because it requires two streams a specialist does not both own. It is the quantified answer to the SCQA's question — the compounding is real, not a story, and it is measurable as the fraction of served feature value that lives only on joins.

sections.heading: Mechanism 2.2 — The flywheel network graph: the coherency centrepiece
sections.text:
If the Sankey shows how much each domain feeds the moat, the network graph shows that every feed is also a return — the loop that makes the wheel a wheel. This is the single most important picture in the section: the six domains as nodes, the moat as the hub, and directed edges in both directions (raw data in, models out).

S-607: the graph is directed and the strategic content is in the return edges. D1 sends traces and receives routing + safety models that raise density D by cutting deadhead miles ~11%. D4 sends fraud/settlement signals and receives a credit model that raises trust T, which is what lets D1 and D3 run cash-free. D5 sends proprietary sensor telemetry — the breadth B no competitor holds — and receives OTA/health models. Delete the hub and the picture collapses into six unconnected nodes: that is the visual proof of the governing thought. The conglomerate discount is the market pricing the six nodes; the flywheel is the edges the market cannot see on a sum-of-the-parts basis.

Remove D6 and the graph is six isolated nodes.

sections.heading: Mechanism 2.3 — Compounding shows up as a rising blended contribution
sections.text: The flywheel's output is economic, and the number is the one the whole document is anchored to: blended contribution/interaction from −0.31 over ten years. Mechanism 2.3 attributes that swing to the moat. S-608: each 10% rise in served feature value (mostly the cross-join band) lowers the marginal cost of a mobility decision (fewer empty miles), a logistics match (higher fill), and a payment authorisation (fewer false declines) simultaneously — because all three read the same improved features. The compounding is that one investment in breadth pays out across four domains at once, which is why Domain 6's −$420m is not a cost but the price of the multiplier. Driver 3 is how that feature value becomes decisions.

sections.heading: Driver 3 — The models: turning compounded breadth into decisions that move D, L, and T
sections.text: Features are potential; models are the realised moat. Driver 3 is the layer where breadth B is converted into density D (routing), liquidity L (matching), and trust T (scoring, safety). The distinct components here are the model-performance charts — ROC curves and a confusion matrix with real figures — because the strategic claim ("the models are better because the data is broader") is only credible if the performance is shown, not asserted.

sections.heading: Mechanism 3.1 — A shared model registry so one improvement propagates to five domains
sections.text: S-609: models are not owned by domains; they are registered in a shared registry (S-611 lineage) and consumed via the serving API. The routing model, the matching model, and the fraud model all draw from the same feature tables, so an improvement to feat_location.demand_z — say from adding D2 charge-gap data — lifts all three at once. This is the mechanism behind "sequence, don't silo": a siloed org retrains three models three times on three partial datasets; the platform retrains the feature once and all models inherit it.

sections.heading: Mechanism 3.2 — Model performance demonstrably tracks data breadth
sections.text:
The claim that breadth causes performance is testable, and Domain 6 tests it by ablation: train each model on a single-domain feature set, then on the full cross-joined set, and measure the lift. The ROC and confusion figures below are the fraud/trust model (the D4 coupling, since it is the cleanest labelled problem).

S-610: the AUC moves from 0.883 (D4 data only) to 0.947 (full cross-domain) and, at a fixed 0.5% false-positive rate, recall rises from 71.2% to 89.9% — an +18.7-point improvement that comes entirely from joining D1 mobility regularity and D5 device-health features onto the D4 label. That delta is the flywheel expressed inside a single confusion matrix: the same false decline the specialist eats, Kepler catches, because the specialist does not hold the mobility and device streams. Higher recall at fixed FPR is directly trust T — fewer fraud losses and fewer false declines — served straight back into D4 approval rates.

sections.heading: Mechanism 3.3 — A feature pipeline that any domain can extend but not fork
sections.text:
The models are only as trustworthy as the features feeding them, so the pipeline is code, versioned and shared. S-612: a domain adds a feature by contributing a transform to the shared pipeline, which enforces point-in-time correctness and train/serve parity; it cannot fork a private copy. The pipeline below computes the cross-domain road_roughness feature (S-605) that three domains consume.

The feature is authored once and read by three domains; the quality gate on sensor_health is the D5 coupling made executable. Driver 3 has turned breadth into decisions; Driver 4 is why those decisions are defensible over a decade.
code: # feature: road_roughness  (S-605, S-612) — joins D1 traces + D5 telemetry + D3 courier GPS@feature(store="feat_location", grain="geohash7x15min", pit=True)  # point-in-time enforceddef road_roughness(ctx):    d1 = ctx.stream("D1.ride")   .select("geohash7", "t", "speed_var")    d5 = ctx.stream("D5.device") .select("geohash7", "t", "accel_rms", "sensor_health")    d3 = ctx.stream("D3.courier").select("geohash7", "t", "gps_jitter")    j  = pit_join([d1, d5, d3], on=["geohash7", "t"], window="15min")   # no leakage    j  = j.filter(col("sensor_health") > 0.8)                           # D5 quality gate    rough = (0.5*z(j.accel_rms) + 0.3*z(j.speed_var) + 0.2*z(j.gps_jitter))    return rough.clip(-4, 4).alias("road_roughness")   # served to D1 routing, D2 siting, D3 ETA

sections.heading: Driver 4 — The moat compounding: the M identity made real, and made uncatchable
sections.text: The final driver is the thesis itself. Drivers 1–3 build pipes, compound breadth, and serve models; Driver 4 states why the result is a moat — an advantage that widens rather than erodes — and ties every term of M to a mechanism above.

sections.heading: Mechanism 4.1 — Each term of M is moved by a named mechanism, and D6 multiplies them
sections.text:
M is a product, and Domain 6 is the multiplication. The table below is the accounting: which term, which mechanism, which cross-feed, and the trajectory.

S-613: because M is multiplicative, moving B by 2.4× while the other terms rise 1.5–1.9× does not add — it multiplies, so M compounds far faster than any single term. This is the mathematical core of "one flywheel, not six": a conglomerate adds P&Ls (linear); the flywheel multiplies moat terms (super-linear). Domain 6 is the only layer where the multiplication physically happens, on shared features.

sections.heading: Mechanism 4.2 — The moat widens with scale, so sequencing beats focus
sections.text: S-614: the cross-join uplift (Sankey's +19%, ROC's +18.7 pts) is a function of how many streams share a key, so it grows every time a domain is sequenced onto the platform — a focused specialist adding users gets more of the same data, while Kepler adding a domain gets a new dimension that multiplies against all existing ones. This is the edge case the bears miss: at small scale the conglomerate discount is correct (six mediocre verticals), but the moat term B has increasing returns, so there is a crossover beyond which the flywheel dominates focus. Sequencing is choosing when to cross it. S-615: the ordering matters because breadth-heavy domains (D5 devices, D1 mobility) must land before the models that monetise them (D4 credit, D3 matching), or the models train on thin data and the compounding stalls — the platform is why the sequence has a right answer.

sections.heading: Mechanism 4.3 — The cost centre is the moat's price, and it is falling per unit of M
sections.text: S-616: Domain 6 costs −420m is not evidence of indiscipline, it is the amortising fixed cost of the multiplier that lifts blended contribution/interaction from −0.31. The bears expense the hub; the thesis capitalises it.

sections.heading: What's true vs what counts
sections.text:
What's true. Domain 6 is, on today's books, a −$420m cost centre that owns no customer and sells no product. Each of the other five domains, run alone, trails a focused specialist — and the market's ~35% conglomerate discount is a rational read of a sum-of-the-parts that genuinely does not clear. The platform is early: feature coverage is uneven across the 42 cities, the cross-join uplift is largest in the three dense metros and thin elsewhere, and several return-edge models (D2 siting, D5 health) are still below the performance bar their data could support. Nothing here says the six businesses are individually excellent. They are not.

What counts. M is a product, not a sum, and Domain 6 is the only layer where the terms are multiplied on shared features rather than optimised in six warehouses. The compounding is measurable and it is not a story: +19% of served feature value lives only on cross-domain joins a specialist cannot assemble, and that band grows every time a domain is sequenced onto the platform; the fraud/trust model's recall rises 71% → 90% at fixed FPR purely from joining D1 and D5 features onto D4 labels; breadth B is on a 2.4× path while D, L, and T rise 1.5–1.9×, and because M multiplies, the moat compounds super-linearly. Delete the hub and the flywheel network graph collapses into six isolated nodes — which is exactly the company the market is pricing. The decisive fact is that the return edges are invisible on a sum-of-the-parts basis and dominant on a flywheel basis, and the whole ten-year swing from −0.31 per interaction is the hub turning. Run Domain 6 as a cost to be cut and the moat never forms. Fund it as the multiplier, sequence the five to feed it in the right order, and it becomes the one thing a focused competitor structurally cannot copy: not a better business, but the edges between all of them.

sections.heading: Synthesis — the flywheel reassembled
sections.text: The six domains were argued separately; the thesis is that they do not act separately. This section reassembles them into the single machine — shows the moat identity compounding from the cross-feeds, sequences the build so the compounding actually happens, stress-tests it against a world that does not cooperate, prices the discount it closes, and reduces the whole ten years to the one dependency that decides success.

sections.heading: The flywheel — how six feeders compound one moat
sections.text:
Read M = D × B × L × T not as an equation to admire but as a wiring diagram. Each domain lifts one or two terms and hands its data to the others, so the terms multiply instead of adding:

Mobility (D1) drives Density to the ceiling — the most physical-world interactions per user of any surface — and hands its location and behaviour exhaust to every other domain: energy siting, logistics routing, fraud detection, device telemetry, and every model.

Devices (D5) drive Breadth — proprietary sensor streams no competitor can buy — and feed them straight into the models.

Logistics (D3) drives Liquidity — the two-sided network that thickens with every courier and merchant — routed by mobility's data and cleared by payments.

Energy (D2) and Payments (D4) own Trust from two directions: energy by never failing at the plug, payments by never failing with money — and payments closes the loop by making mobility and logistics cash-free, which raises the interaction count that lifts Density again.

Data/AI (D6) is the hub: it ingests all five, and serves models back that make all five cheaper, safer, and stickier — which produces more data, which improves the models. That is the loop.

The compounding is the difference between the terms added (a conglomerate: D + B + L + T, four mediocre numbers) and multiplied (a flywheel: D × B × L × T, where every feeder lifts the whole product). It is why the break-up case is not merely suboptimal but value-destroying: spin the domains apart and you sever the cross-feeds, the terms stop multiplying, and each orphaned business reverts to the mediocre specialist it always was on its own. The moat is not in any domain. It is in the wiring.

sections.heading: Sequencing — the order is the strategy
sections.text:
If the moat is in the wiring, then the strategy is the order in which you lay the wire. Build the domains in the wrong sequence and each arrives before the data that would make it work; build them in the right sequence and each arrives on top of a moat the previous ones already thickened.

The load-bearing decision is Phase I: the Data/AI platform is built first, as infrastructure, not as a business — even though it has no P&L and loses $420m a year today. Sequence it last (the intuitive order — "prove the businesses, then connect them") and every domain spends years hoarding data that never compounds, the terms only ever add, and Kepler is the mediocre conglomerate the market already prices. The counter-intuitive order is the whole thesis: build the hub before the spokes.
tables: The hub must exist before any cross-feed can flow; without the feature store and serving layer, the other five hoard data and the terms only add. This is the critical path.
Mobility maxes Density and starts the location-data firehose; energy lowers its cost floor and starts earning Trust. Density is the term every later domain multiplies against.

Payments needs D1/D3 behaviour data to underwrite and needs the interaction base to be worth closing cash-free; arriving now, it monetises a moat that already exists rather than trying to manufacture one.
Logistics thickens Liquidity on top of mobility's routing and payments' rails; devices deepen Breadth once the models (D6) are good enough to exploit new sensor streams.
All feeds live; the moat compounds on its own; the work becomes defending the flywheel and extending it to new cities and surfaces.

sections.heading: Scenarios — does the flywheel spin if the world resists?
sections.text:
The +$0.31 contribution target assumes the compounding lands. It is a distribution, not a forecast, hinging on the two forces Kepler does not control — the regulatory posture toward platform data-sharing (which governs whether the cross-feeds are even legal) and the cost of capital (which governs how long Kepler can fund a hub that loses money before it compounds):

Base (≈50%): data-sharing tolerated, capital patient. The cross-feeds wire up on plan, the moat compounds, contribution reaches +$0.31, and the conglomerate discount inverts to a platform premium by year 8. The flywheel case is vindicated.

Bull (≈20%): permissive regulation, cheap capital. The cross-feeds run at full strength and Kepler compounds faster than any single-vertical competitor can respond; contribution reaches +$0.48 and the moat becomes genuinely uncatchable — but the risk is regulatory backlash provoked by that very dominance, so the bull case carries the seed of the bear.

Bear (≈30%): data-sharing restricted, capital dear. Privacy regulation walls the domains off from each other — the cross-feeds become illegal, the terms revert to adding — and expensive capital forces Kepler to shut the loss-making hub before it compounds. Contribution stalls at −$0.02, roughly breakeven, and the break-up case wins by default. This is the real risk, and it is not operational — it is regulatory: the flywheel's legality, not its engineering, is the fragile part.

The probability-weighted contribution is ≈ +$0.24, and the asymmetry is the point: the upside is a platform premium and an uncatchable moat; the downside is reverting to the conglomerate Kepler is already priced as — so the flywheel strategy is close to a free option on the compounding, provided the regulatory risk is managed rather than ignored.

sections.heading: The discount it closes
sections.text: The ~35% conglomerate discount is not a market error to be argued away; it is the market correctly pricing what it can see — six mediocre businesses and no visible connective tissue. The discount closes only when the cross-feeds become visible and measurable — when Kepler can show, cross-feed by cross-feed, that mobility's data measurably lowered energy's siting cost, that device sensors measurably sharpened fraud models, that the terms are multiplying, not adding. The strategy's financial objective is therefore not a margin target; it is making the flywheel legible — instrumenting and reporting the compounding so the market re-rates the connective tissue from zero to the asset it actually is. Contribution per interaction (−0.31) is the single headline metric of that legibility.

sections.heading: The one dependency
sections.text: Everything above hangs on one thing, and it is the same thing the sequencing already named: the Data/AI platform (D6) must be built first and funded through its loss-making years as infrastructure, before any domain's cross-feed can flow. It is the hub; without it, the spokes never connect, the terms only add, and the entire flywheel thesis collapses into the conglomerate it was meant to escape. Every other decision in this document can slip a year; this one cannot, and it is the one the market — and an impatient board staring at a −$420m cost centre — will be most tempted to cut. Cutting it is not a cost saving. It is choosing to be a conglomerate.

sections.heading: What's true, and what counts
sections.text:
What's settled. The six businesses are real and at scale; the conglomerate discount is real; the moat identity's four terms are each measurable; and the cross-feeds are technically demonstrable domain by domain. None of that is in dispute.

What counts. The one decision that determines the decade is whether the board funds Kepler as a flywheel — hub first, sequenced, cross-feeds instrumented and made legible — or as six businesses to be optimised or spun apart. The first multiplies four terms into an uncatchable moat and inverts the discount; the second severs the wiring and confirms the mediocrity the discount already prices. The difference between them is not capital or engineering. It is whether Kepler believes its own thesis enough to build the hub before the spokes.

sections.heading: Evidence register
sections.text:
Traces are grouped by domain so any claim walks back to its source; the domain sections carry the detailed S-1xx…S-6xx traces inline.

S-0xx — thesis & moat. The M = D×B×L×T identity, the −0.31 contribution target, the 35% conglomerate discount, the scenario distribution, the sequencing critical path. Source: strategy & the unit-economics model.

S-1xx — Mobility. Fleet density, utilization, safety, rider economics, the location-data cross-feeds. Fleet & safety systems of record.

S-2xx — Energy. Network utilization, dispatch, reliability, the transition scenario. Grid & operations.

S-3xx — Logistics. Two-sided liquidity, courier/merchant cohorts, matching, density. Marketplace analytics.

S-4xx — Payments. Rails, underwriting, fraud/compliance, interchange. Fintech & risk.

S-5xx — Devices. Product, BOM/margin, supply chain, sensor-data breadth. Hardware & supply chain.

S-6xx — Data/AI. Architecture, the data flywheel, model performance, the moat compounding. Platform group.

Note on verification: figures are internal measured facts from named systems of record. The genuinely uncertain inputs are the two exogenous scenario drivers — the regulatory posture toward platform data-sharing and the cost of capital — presented as a distribution because they are not knowable, and never asserted as forecasts.
